source_metadata.title_raw: Marc G. Hynes - A-19-030 - June 6, 2019 - Los Altos

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 6, 2019

Marc G. Hynes
Atkinson Farasyn, LLP
5050 El Camino Real, Suite 205
Los Altos, CA 94022

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-030

Dear Mr. Hynes:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Saratoga Fire Protection
District ("District") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act")¹ and Government Code section 1090 ("Section 1090"). Please note that we are only providing
advice under these conflict of interest provisions and not under other general conflict of interest
prohibitions. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this
is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Santa Clara County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requester." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit District
Commissioner Joe Long ("Commissioner Long") from taking part in decisions concerning revisions
to a Joint Use Agreement between the District and the Saratoga Federated Church (the "Church")
with respect to a parking lot shared by both entities that may result in fewer public parking spaces
in the lot?

2. Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Commissioner
Joe Long from taking part in decisions concerning an agreement between the District and the
Church to swap parcels of land that make up a parking lot shared by both?

_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-030
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Long from taking part in
decisions concerning revisions to the Joint Use Agreement between the District and the Church.

2. No. Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibit Commissioner Joe Long from taking part
in decisions concerning an agreement between the District and the Church to swap parcels of land
that make up the shared parking lot.

FACTS

You are counsel for the District, which is an independent fire district organized and
operating pursuant to the Fire Protection District Law of 1987. The District Board of Directors
consists of three members, including Commissioner Long.

The District owns and maintains real property in the City of Saratoga generally located at
the intersection of Saratoga Avenue and the Saratoga-Los Gatos Road. Immediately adjacent to the
District's property is land owned by the Saratoga Federated Church. Under the existing Joint Use
Agreement, the District and Church for several years have shared the parking area along with the
expenses for lighting and landscape irrigation related to the parking lot.

To the casual observer, the parking lot appears to be one piece of property being used as a
parking lot between Church buildings and the District's fire station. However, there is a patchwork
of ownership with the Church owning certain parcels while the District owns others. The District
and the Church would like to swap parcels so that District-owned land is adjacent to and serving the
District as much as possible with the same being true for the Church.²

There are 10 spaces in the parking lot that the City requires be made available for public
use. The Church is on its own behalf may approach the City to request a reduction in the amount of
public spaces the City requires for the parking lot. Should the Church obtain approval to reduce the
number of public spaces, the District may then have to participate in the determination of the
specific number of public spaces to eliminate when the Joint Use Agreement is revised.³ If the
number of public spaces were reduced, people would likely have to park on the street unless they
are parishioners or intending to visit the Church.

Commissioner Long has a financial interest in three parcels of real property within a 500-
foot radius of the parking lot. The interests are as follows:

1. Mr. Long, as trustee and beneficiary of his deceased parents' Trust, has a one-third

_______________
² In addition, the District has approached the Department of Transportation seeking a transfer of ownership of
two small state-owned parcels that are part of the existing parking lot area. These parcels would be part of the land swap
between the District and the Church.

³ Although the revision of the existing agreement, which does not cover the 10 public parking spaces, will
principally involve the adjustment of responsibilities for maintaining lighting and landscaping, it may eliminate some
public parking spaces if the City provides such authorization to the Church.

File No. A-19-030
Page No. 3

ownership of an office building located at 14363 Saratoga Avenue. Rental income from this
property is currently $12,000 per year.

2. Mr. Long and his wife jointly own a residential condominium located at 14347A
Saratoga Avenue. Rental income from this property is currently $33,600 per year.

3. Mr. Long and his wife own a 50% interest in a residential home and small office
located at 20390 Orchard Road. Their share of gross income from this property is currently $20,000
per year.

All of Commissioner Long's properties have adequate parking; the office building has more
parking than required; the condominiums have all required garages and parking spaces; and the
house has a garage, driveway, and street parking while the attached office has street parking. There
is no evidence that the parcels of property in which Commissioner Long has an interest use or enjoy
any part of the parking lot.

You state that even assuming all 10 public spaces were eliminated from the parking lot,
there is generally sufficient street parking such that the street parking for all of Commissioner
Long's properties would not be impacted. You also state that the District's acquisition of the two
small state parcels would have no impact on the amount of parking in the parking lot or on the
street.

ANALYSIS

The Act

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that may give rise to an
official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include
all of the following:

• An interest in any business entity in which the official has an investment of $2,000 or more
(Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is an employee or holds a position of
management (Section 87103(d)). Commissioner Long has business interests in renting out
an office building, a home with an attached office, and a condominium.

• An interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).) Commissioner Long has a real property interest in all three properties.4

4 Commissioner Long has a real property interest in the office building located at 14363 Saratoga Avenue due
to his pro rata beneficial interest in the Trust. An "interest in real property" includes a pro rata share of real property
held in a trust, in which the official owns, directly or indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section
82033.) An official has a "direct, indirect or beneficial interest" in a trust where, as here, he is a beneficiary and
presently receives income. (Regulation 18234(c)(2).)

File No. A-19-030
Page No. 4

• An interest in any source of income aggregating $500 or more to the official in the 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Commissioner Long has a source-of-
income interest in all three properties and in any tenant of those properties who has paid rent
of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decisions.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official
or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved
in a decision, as here, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable.
In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected
absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property in which an official has a financial interest is material under
certain enumerated circumstances. The regulation provides that the effect is material whenever the
governmental decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the
parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable
impact on the official's property..." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).)

Here, Commissioner Long has a real property interest in three properties that are all within
500 feet of the parking lot used by the District and the Church. According to your facts, the District
and the Church would like to swap ownership of the various parcels of land that make up the
parking lot they share in order to clean up the current patchwork of ownership. In addition, they
would like to revise their current Joint Use Agreement, principally involving responsibilities for
maintaining the parking lot lighting and landscaping, that may result in an undetermined number of
the ten public parking spaces being reduced.

Because the properties are within 500 feet of the property involved in the decision, the effect
of the decisions is presumed both foreseeable and material under the applicable regulations unless
there is clear and convincing evidence that the agreement to swap land and/or revise the Joint Use
Agreement would have no measurable impact on Commissioner Long's property interests, which
are all within 500 feet of the parking lot. Additionally, Regulation 18702(b) provides that "the
financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or
insignificant."

You stated that all of Commissioner Long's properties have adequate parking: the office
building possibly has more parking than required; the condominiums have all required garages and
parking spaces; and the house has a garage, driveway, and street parking while the attached office
has street parking. In addition, you stated there is no evidence that the parcels of property in which
Commissioner Long has an interest use or enjoy any part of the parking lot.

File No. A-19-030
Page No. 5

A reduction in public parking spaces may require some individuals who normally use the lot
to park on the street. However, you state that even assuming all 10 public spaces were to be
eliminated, there is generally sufficient street parking adjacent to the parking lot such that the street
parking for all of Commissioner Long's properties would not be impacted.

Therefore, based on these specific facts, it does not appear the financial effect of the Joint
Use Agreement on Commissioner Long's real property interests will likely be more than nominal,
inconsequential, or insignificant. The same is true with the potential transfer of ownership between
the District and the Church of the various land parcels that make up the parking lot.⁵

Business Entity

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides the materiality standard for an interest in a business entity,
including a source of income, not explicitly involved in the decision (see Regulation 18702.3).
Under Regulation 18702.1(b), a decision's effect on an official's business interest is material "if a
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the ... value of a privately-held business
entity."

Here, there are no facts to indicate that the financial effect of either the land swap involving
the parking lot parcels or revision of the existing Joint Use Agreement between the District and
Church will impact the value of the businesses in which Commissioner Long is interested.

Accordingly, the Act's conflict of interest provisions would not prohibit Commissioner
Long from taking part in decisions concerning the transfer of land ownership between the District
and the Church⁶ or the revision of the current Joint Use Agreement.

Section 1090

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or
minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided
allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565,
569.)

The determinative issue here is whether Commissioner Long has a prohibitive financial
interest in any of the potential agreements discussed above.

⁵ The parcels of land include two small parcels of the parking area that the District will separately ask the State
to transfer to it. For all of the same reasons, the financial effect of the transfer of those land parcels from the State to the
District will not be more than nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant with respect to Commissioner Long's real
property interests.

⁶ This includes the initial transfer of two small parking lot parcels from the State to the District.

File No. A-19-030
Page No. 6

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest." (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Officials are deemed to have a financial
interest in a contract if they will profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 1090 does not
specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state that
prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or
the possibility of losses, as well as-the prospect of pecuniary gain. (People v. Vallerga (1977) 67
Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn.5; Terry v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; 85
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162 (2001).)

Here, the District may be potentially involved in the making of three agreements involving
the parking area including an initial transfer of two small parcels of the parking lot from the State to
the District, the swap of parking lot parcels with the Church, and the revision of the existing Joint
Use Agreement with the Church.

You have provided no facts indicating that Commissioner Long stands to incur any financial
profit or loss from the potential agreements. Although revision of the Joint Use Agreement may
reduce some of the public parking spaces in the lot, the facts do not suggest that any reduction will
impact Commissioner Long's property or business interests.

Accordingly, based on these specific facts, Commissioner Long does not have a financial
interest under Section 1090 in any of the potential agreements discussed above.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit District 
Commissioner Joe Long ("Commissioner Long") from taking part in decisions concerning revisions 
to a Joint Use Agreement between the District and the Saratoga Federated Church (the "Church") 
with respect to a parking lot shared by both entities that may result in fewer public parking spaces 
in the lot? 
2. Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Commissioner 
Joe Long from taking part in decisions conc,erning an agreement between the District and the 
Church to swap parcels of land that make up a parking lot shared by both?
sections.conclusion:
FileNo. A-19-030  
1. No. Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Long from taking part in 
decisions concerning revisions to the Joint Use Agreement between the District and the Church. 
2. No. Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibit Commissioner Joe Long from taking part 
in decisions concerning an agreement between the District and the Church to swap parcels of land 
that make up the shared parking lot.

The Act 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the decision's effect 
on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) The financial interests that may give rise to an 
official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include 
all of the following: 
• An interest in any business entity in which the official has an investment of $2,000 or more 
(Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is an employee or holds a position of 
management (Section 87103(d)). Commissioner Long has business interests in renting out 
an office building, a home with an attached office, and a condominium. 
• An interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) Commissioner Long has a real property interest in all three properties.4 
4 Commissioner Long has a real property interest in the office building located at 14363 Saratoga Avenue due 
to his 33 percent beneficial interest in the Trust. An "interest in real property" includes a pro rata share ofreal property 
held in a trust, in which the official owns, directly or indirectly or beneficially, a IO percent interest or greater. (Section 
82033.) An official has a "direct, indirect or beneficial interest" in a trust where, as here, he is a beneficiary and 
presently receives income. (Regulation 18234(c)(2).) 


FileNo. A-19-030  
• An interest in any source of income aggregating $5Q0 or more to the official in the 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Commissioner Long has a source-of-
income interest in all three properties and in any tenant of those properties who has paid rent 
of $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the decisions.

In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected 
absent extraordinary circumstances·not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Real Property 
Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on real property in which an official has a financial interest is material under 
certain enumerated circumstances. The regulation provides that the effect is material whenever the

parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable

Here, Commissioner Long has a real property interest in three properties that are all within

parking lot they share in order to clean up the current patchwork of ownership. In addition, they 
would like to revise their current Joint Use Agreement, principally involving responsibilities for 
maintaining the parking lot lighting and landscaping, that may result in an undetermined number of 
the ten public parking spaces being reduced. 
Because the properties are within 500 feet of the property involved in the decision, the effect 
of the decisions is presumed both foreseeable and material under the applicable regulations unless 
there is clear and convincing evidence that the agreement to swap land and/or revise the Joint Use 
Agreement would have no measurable impact on Commissioner Long's property interests, which 
are all within 500 feet of the parking lot. Additionally, Regulation 18702(b) provides that "the 
financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or 
insignificant." 
You stated that all of Commissioner Long's properties have adequate parking: the office 
building possibly has more parking than required; the condominiums have all required garages and 
parking spaces; and the house has a garage, driveway, and street parking while the attached office 
has street parking. In addition, you stated there is no evidence that the parcels of property in which 
Commissioner Long has an interest use or enjoy any part of the parking lot. 


FileNo. A-19-030  
A reduction in public parking spaces may require some individuals who normally use the lot 
to park on the street. However, you state that even assuming all 10 public spaces were to be 
eliminated, there is generally sufficient street parking adjacent to the parking lot such that the street 
parking for all of Commissioner Long's properties would not be impacted. 
Therefore, based on these specific facts, it does not appear the financial effect of the Joint 
Use Agreement on Commissioner Long's real property interests will likely be more than nominal, 
inconsequential, or insignificant. The same is true with the potential transfer of ownership between 
the District and the Church of the various land parcels that make up the parking lot. 5 
Business Entity 
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides the materiality standard for an interest in a business entity, 
including a source of income, not explicitly involved in the decision (see Regulation 18702.3). 
Under Regulation 18702.l(b), a decision's effect on an official's business interest is material "if a 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the ... value of a privately-held business 
entity." 
Here, there are no facts to indicate that the financial effect of either the land swap involving

Church will impact the value of the businesses in which Commissioner Long is interested.

Long from taking part in decisions concerning the transfer of land ownership between the District 
and the Church6 or the revision of the current Joint Use Agreement.

from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of

allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565,

District will not be more than nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant with respect to Commissioner Long's real 
property interests. 
6 This includes the initial transfer of two small parking lot parcels from the State to the District. 


FileNo. A-19-030  
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333.) Officials are deemed to have a financial 
interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 1090 does not 
specifically define the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General opinions state that

You have provided no facts indicating that Commissioner Long stands to incur any financial

impact Commissioner Long's property or business interests. 
Accordingly, based on these specific facts, Commissioner Long does not have a financial 
interest under Section 1090 in any of the potential agreements discussed above.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit District 
Commissioner Joe Long ("Commissioner Long") from taking part in decisions concerning revisions 
to a Joint Use Agreement between the District and the Saratoga Federated Church (the "Church") 
with respect to a parking lot shared by both entities that may result in fewer public parking spaces 
in the lot? 
2. Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit Commissioner 
Joe Long from taking part in decisions conc,erning an agreement between the District and the 
Church to swap parcels of land that make up a parking lot shared by both?

CONCLUSION: FileNo. A-19-030  
1. No. Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Long from taking part in 
decisions concerning revisions to the Joint Use Agreement between the District and the Church. 
2. No. Neither the Act nor Section 1090 prohibit Commissioner Joe Long from taking part 
in decisions concerning an agreement between the District and the Church to swap parcels of land 
that make up the shared parking lot.

All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.
